questions.question_text: An Indiana broker discovers during a listing walkthrough that the home has an active termite infestation. The seller instructs the broker to say nothing. The broker must:
questions.correct_answer: Disclose the infestation to prospective buyers despite the seller's instruction
distractors: Follow the seller's instruction — the seller controls disclosure
Disclose only if asked directly by a buyer
Withdraw from the listing rather than disclose
questions.explanation: Under IC 25-34.1-10-10(d), Indiana brokers must disclose to prospective buyers adverse material facts actually known concerning the physical condition of the property, regardless of seller instructions — an active termite infestation is a classic latent defect. A seller cannot instruct a broker to withhold it; the statute's confidentiality duty expressly gives way where disclosure is required by law. While withdrawal is an option, the required act is disclosure.

questions.question_text: Under Indiana law, which of the following is a material fact that a broker must disclose?
questions.correct_answer: A known structural defect in the foundation
distractors: The seller's reason for moving out of state
The buyer's maximum loan approval amount
A neighbor's plans to landscape their yard
questions.explanation: A known structural defect directly affects the property's value and desirability and is a clear material fact requiring disclosure. The seller's reason for relocating is confidential client information. The buyer's financial capacity is not a material fact about the property. A neighbor's landscaping plans do not affect the subject property's physical condition.

questions.question_text: An Indiana buyer's broker is aware that a property being shown has a history of basement flooding, though the seller did not disclose it. The buyer's broker learned this from a neighbor. The broker should:
questions.correct_answer: Disclose the flooding history to the buyer as a known material fact
distractors: Stay silent — the broker cannot verify information from a neighbor
Ask the listing broker to disclose it on the broker's behalf
Wait for the inspection report to confirm the issue
questions.explanation: Indiana brokers must disclose known material facts affecting the property. If a broker has reason to believe a material defect exists — even from a third-party source — the broker should disclose it to the buyer and note the source. Waiting for inspection or staying silent could harm the buyer and expose the broker to liability.

questions.question_text: A property in Indiana was the site of a homicide 10 years ago. A buyer asks the broker about the home's history. Under Indiana law:
questions.correct_answer: The broker is not required to disclose the homicide, but may not misrepresent the facts if asked directly
distractors: The broker must proactively disclose all stigmatizing events
The broker may legally deny knowledge of the homicide
The broker must refuse to answer to avoid liability
questions.explanation: Under Indiana's psychologically affected properties statute (IC 32-21-6-5 and -6), an owner or agent is not required to disclose that a property was the site of a homicide and is not even liable for refusing to disclose it when asked — but may not intentionally misrepresent a fact about the property in response to a direct inquiry. Denying knowledge the broker actually has would be an intentional misrepresentation. Refusing to answer is permitted but not required.

questions.question_text: An Indiana seller's broker is reviewing a purchase agreement when the broker notices that the buyer's financing contingency has already expired. The broker does not inform the seller. This is:
questions.correct_answer: A failure to disclose a material fact affecting the transaction
distractors: Acceptable — the broker is not responsible for tracking contingency dates
Acceptable if the buyer's broker was notified instead
A violation only after closing has occurred
questions.explanation: Knowing that a financing contingency has expired and not informing the seller is a failure to disclose a material fact about the transaction status. The seller's broker owes the client material information relevant to the transaction. This is true regardless of whether the buyer's broker was separately notified.

questions.question_text: An Indiana broker wants to purchase a property listed by another broker in the firm. The broker must first:
questions.correct_answer: Disclose their licensed status and personal interest to the seller
distractors: Obtain managing broker approval before making an offer
Resign their license before purchasing any property
Use a family member's name on the offer to avoid the issue
questions.explanation: Indiana requires brokers to disclose their licensed status and personal financial interest when purchasing property. This protects the seller from being disadvantaged by dealing with a trained professional without knowing it. Using a family member's name to avoid disclosure would constitute fraud. Managing broker approval may be prudent but is not the primary legal requirement.

questions.question_text: An Indiana broker owns a 25% interest in a property management company. A buyer client asks the broker for a property management referral. The broker recommends the company without disclosing the ownership interest. This is:
questions.correct_answer: A violation — the financial interest must be disclosed to the client
distractors: Acceptable as a general business referral
Acceptable if the management company is fully licensed
A violation only if the client pays a referral fee
questions.explanation: Indiana requires brokers to disclose personal financial interests in any business they recommend to clients. A 25% ownership stake in a property management company is a direct financial interest that must be disclosed before making the referral. This applies regardless of whether the company is licensed or whether the broker receives a referral fee.

questions.question_text: An Indiana broker is selling their own home and has the listing on the MLS. The broker's personal interest disclosure obligation requires:
questions.correct_answer: Disclosing the licensed status prominently in all advertising and to all buyers
distractors: No disclosure — FSBO sales do not require license disclosure
Disclosure only when a buyer makes a written offer
Disclosure only in the purchase agreement, not in ads
questions.explanation: When an Indiana broker sells their own property, they must disclose their licensed status in advertising and to buyers — not only at the offer stage. Indiana's advertising rules and personal interest disclosure rules both apply. Buyers have a right to know they are dealing with a trained professional when considering the transaction.

questions.question_text: An Indiana broker represents a seller and also has a financial interest in the title company the broker recommends. The broker discloses this to the seller in the listing agreement. Is this sufficient?
questions.correct_answer: Yes — written disclosure to the affected party satisfies the requirement
distractors: No — the broker must refer to a competing title company instead
No — the IPLA must be notified of the financial interest
Yes — but only if the managing broker also signs the disclosure
questions.explanation: Indiana's personal interest disclosure requirement is satisfied when the financial interest is disclosed in writing to the affected party. The seller is free to choose any title company after being informed. The IPLA does not need notification for disclosed financial interests, and the managing broker's cosignature is not required. Note that because a title company is a settlement service provider, federal law (RESPA) separately requires an affiliated business arrangement disclosure at or before the referral, and the client remains free to choose any title company.

questions.question_text: An Indiana broker sells a property to a relative at below-market value without disclosing the family relationship to the seller. This situation most likely involves a violation of:
questions.correct_answer: Personal interest disclosure — the broker's relationship is a material interest
distractors: Advertising rules — the price must be publicly disclosed
Limited agency rules — both parties must consent in writing
No violation if the sale price was agreed to by the seller
questions.explanation: The broker's family relationship with the buyer is a personal interest that creates a potential conflict and must be disclosed to the seller. Even if the seller agreed to the price, the failure to disclose the relationship is a separate violation. Seller agreement does not cure the lack of disclosure of a material personal interest.

questions.question_text: Which Indiana agency enforces the state's fair housing law?
distractors: The Indiana Real Estate Commission (IREC)
The Indiana Professional Licensing Agency
The Indiana Attorney General's office
questions.explanation: Indiana's fair housing law is enforced by the Indiana Civil Rights Commission (ICRC), not IREC or the IPLA. IREC handles real estate license violations, while the ICRC specifically investigates housing discrimination complaints. The Attorney General may assist but is not the primary enforcement body.

questions.question_text: Indiana law protects a housing-related class that is NOT specifically listed in the federal Fair Housing Act. Under the Indiana Civil Rights Law (IC 22-9-1), which class is this?
distractors: Sexual orientation
Source of income
Marital status
questions.explanation: Indiana's fair housing statute (IC 22-9.5-5-1) mirrors the federal Act's seven classes exactly. Ancestry protection comes from the Indiana Civil Rights Law (IC 22-9-1), which declares equal opportunity in the acquisition through purchase or rental of real property a civil right and prohibits denial based on ancestry. Federal law protects national origin, which is related but separately enumerated. Sexual orientation, source of income, and marital status are not protected classes under any Indiana statute. The ICRC administers both laws.

questions.question_text: A buyer believes an Indiana broker steered them away from a neighborhood based on their ancestry. The buyer wishes to file a complaint. In Indiana, the complaint must be filed with:
distractors: The Indiana Professional Licensing Agency (IPLA)
The U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD)
The Indiana Real Estate Commission
questions.explanation: Housing discrimination complaints in Indiana are filed with the Indiana Civil Rights Commission (ICRC), which administers both the Indiana Fair Housing Act (IC 22-9.5) and the Indiana Civil Rights Law (IC 22-9-1) — the statute that protects ancestry in real property transactions. IREC/IPLA handle real estate license discipline, and HUD handles federal Fair Housing Act complaints; the ICRC is the correct agency for a state-law housing complaint.

questions.question_text: A tenant believes their landlord violated Indiana's fair housing law. The landlord's conduct occurred on January 1, 2025. The last day the tenant may file a complaint with the ICRC is:
questions.correct_answer: January 1, 2026 — Indiana allows one year from the discriminatory act to file with the ICRC
distractors: June 30, 2025 — housing complaints must be filed within 180 days
January 1, 2027 — Indiana allows two years to file with the ICRC
There is no deadline — housing complaints may be filed at any time
questions.explanation: IC 22-9.5-6-1(c) requires an ICRC complaint to be filed not later than one year after the alleged discriminatory housing practice occurred or terminated, so conduct on January 1, 2025 must be complained of by January 1, 2026. The 180-day window applies to the ICRC's non-housing civil rights complaints (employment, education, public accommodations), not housing. There is no two-year or open-ended administrative window. The federal deadline for filing with HUD is also one year.

questions.question_text: An Indiana property manager refuses to rent a unit to an applicant because of their Eastern European ancestry. Under Indiana law, this refusal is:
questions.correct_answer: Prohibited — ancestry is protected under the Indiana Civil Rights Law, and the refusal is also national origin discrimination
distractors: Permitted — ancestry is not covered by Indiana law
Permitted if the manager applies the policy equally to all applicants
Prohibited only if the applicant can prove discriminatory intent
questions.explanation: The Indiana Civil Rights Law (IC 22-9-1) prohibits denying equal opportunity in the purchase or rental of real property based on ancestry, and the same refusal would constitute national origin discrimination under the Indiana Fair Housing Act (IC 22-9.5-5-1) and the federal Fair Housing Act. Applying a discriminatory policy consistently does not make it legal, and discriminatory effect as well as direct evidence can establish a violation.
